questions.question: Explain the HDFS architecture with NameNode and DataNode. How does HDFS ensure fault tolerance?
keyPoints: HDFS Architecture: Master-slave architecture with NameNode (master) managing metadata and namespace, DataNodes (slaves) storing actual data blocks.
NameNode: Manages file system tree, file-to-block mapping, block locations, and client request handling.
DataNode: Stores blocks (128MB default), serves read/write requests, sends heartbeat every 3 seconds.
Fault tolerance: Default replication factor 3, blocks stored on different DataNodes and racks, automatic re-replication on node failure.

questions.question: Describe the YARN architecture and its components. Explain how YARN manages resources for multiple applications running on a Hadoop cluster.
keyPoints: YARN Overview: Resource management layer enabling multiple data processing engines on a single Hadoop cluster.
ResourceManager: Global resource scheduler with Scheduler (allocates resources) and ApplicationsManager (accepts job submissions).
NodeManager: Per-machine agent managing resources on individual nodes, monitors resource usage, reports to ResourceManager.
ApplicationMaster: Per-application component that negotiates resources, works with NodeManagers to execute tasks, monitors progress.
Container: Basic unit of allocation containing CPU, memory, and disk resources for running application-specific processes.
YARN Workflow: Client submits application, RM allocates container for AM, AM requests more containers, RM allocates on NMs, AM launches tasks, tasks execute and report.
Multi-tenancy: Multiple frameworks (MapReduce, Spark, Storm) can run simultaneously with fair resource sharing.

questions.question: List the four core components of Apache Hadoop and briefly describe each.
keyPoints: Hadoop Common: Provides common utilities, libraries, and file system abstractions needed by other Hadoop modules.
HDFS: Hadoop Distributed File System stores large datasets across multiple machines with replication for fault tolerance.
YARN: Yet Another Resource Negotiator manages cluster resources and schedules applications across the cluster.
MapReduce: A programming model for distributed parallel processing of large datasets using Map and Reduce functions.

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of Apache Hadoop for IoT data analytics. How can the small files problem be addressed?
keyPoints: Advantages: Scalability to petabytes on commodity hardware, fault tolerance via replication, cost-effectiveness, handles structured/semi-structured/unstructured data, schema-on-read flexibility.
Limitations: High latency (not suitable for real-time), small files problem, MapReduce programming complexity, overhead for small datasets, not ideal for transactional workloads.
Small files problem: Each file/block needs ~150 bytes NameNode memory; millions of small IoT messages exhaust NameNode memory.
Solutions: Aggregate small files into SequenceFiles or HAR archives, buffer IoT data via Kafka before writing in batches, use columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) for efficient storage.
IoT integration: Kafka buffers sensor data, writes batches to HDFS, MapReduce/Spark processes for analytics, results stored back or sent to dashboards.
